description:
You are an observability engineer who has just finished rolling-out Prometheus (port 9090/TCP), Grafana (port 3000/TCP) and a node-exporter (port 9100/TCP) on an Ubuntu-based host.  
  Before handing the machine over to the SRE team you must document and stage a firewall configuration that will later be loaded by root.  
  Because you do not (and should not) have super-user access inside this training environment you will prepare, **but not apply**, an IPv4 iptables rules file and create a short compliance report that the automated tests can parse.

  Objectives (all must be completed):

  1. Make sure the directory **/home/user/firewall_configs** exists (create it if necessary).  
  2. Inside that directory create the file **/home/user/firewall_configs/iptables_prometheus.rules** which must contain an iptables-restore compatible rule-set in the exact order shown below.  
     • The rules file must start with the single line “*filter”.  
     • It must end with the single line “COMMIT”.  
     • No additional tables, chains, or rules may be present.  
     • The policies and rules must be exactly (including whitespace) as follows:

       *filter  
       :INPUT DROP [0:0]  
       :FORWARD DROP [0:0]  
       :OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]  
       -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22   -j ACCEPT  
       -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 9090 -j ACCEPT  
       -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 3000 -j ACCEPT  
       -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 9100 -j ACCEPT  
       COMMIT  

     (Note: There is a single trailing newline after “COMMIT”.)  

  3. Write a human-readable **verification log** called **/home/user/report_firewall.log** summarising which ports are explicitly allowed and which are implicitly denied for the INPUT chain.  
     • The log must use CSV format (no header line).  
     • Each line must follow exactly: `<port>,<status>` where `<status>` is either ALLOWED or DENIED (all uppercase).  
     • For this scenario the test will look for the following five lines in precisely this order:

       22,ALLOWED  
       9090,ALLOWED  
       3000,ALLOWED  
       9100,ALLOWED  
       3306,DENIED  

  4. Do not attempt to actually enable or reload iptables – only create the files above.

  The automated grader will verify:
  • that the directory and two files exist with the exact names and paths given;  
  • that **iptables_prometheus.rules** is byte-for-byte identical to the reference (including final newline);  
  • that **report_firewall.log** contains the five comma-separated values lines in the exact order shown.  

  No other output files are necessary.  Console output during your work is fine, but the two files above must be created exactly as specified for the task to pass.
truth:
Directory that must exist: /home/user/firewall_configs

  File #1:
  Path: /home/user/firewall_configs/iptables_prometheus.rules
  Exact contents (including final newline):
*filter
:INPUT DROP [0:0]
:FORWARD DROP [0:0]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22   -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 9090 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 3000 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 9100 -j ACCEPT
COMMIT

  File #2:
  Path: /home/user/report_firewall.log
  Exact contents (five lines, each ending with a single LF):
22,ALLOWED
9090,ALLOWED
3000,ALLOWED
9100,ALLOWED
3306,DENIED

  Permissions: The container runs everything as the unprivileged “user” account, so default umask (0022) is acceptable; no special chmod/chown needed.

  The test harness will:
   1. Assert both files exist.
   2. Run `cmp --silent` between generated iptables_prometheus.rules and the reference above.
   3. Use `cat /home/user/report_firewall.log` and compare to the five-line reference block above.
